channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: Is There a Silver Lining Behind The Looming Dark Clouds of Quantum’s Crypto-busting Powers?
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a crucial turning point in quantum computing discourse, where Google's recent advances are being reframed from a pure cybersecurity threat to evidence of accelerating quantum utility across applications. While much attention has focused on quantum cryptography-breaking capabilities creating security risks, the same technological progress suggests that beneficial quantum applications in optimization, simulation, and machine learning may arrive on similar timelines. This dual perspective is important because it shifts the conversation from purely defensive preparations to proactive development of quantum advantages, potentially accelerating investment and research into near-term quantum applications that could deliver economic benefits alongside the well-publicized security challenges.

Insider Brief The alarm over Google Quantum AI’s latest findings has centered on risk, but the same advance is sharpening the case that useful quantum applications may arrive sooner than expected. A recent white paper written by a Google-led research team outlined how a sufficiently large quantum system — on the order of hundreds of […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Palm Beach State College Takes Aim at Quantum Workforce Demand
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a critical bottleneck that could determine quantum computing's real-world success: the workforce pipeline. While quantum research has traditionally required PhD-level expertise, the transition to practical quantum applications will demand a much broader range of technicians, engineers, and support personnel with specialized quantum literacy. Palm Beach State College's initiative represents an important shift toward building the mid-level workforce that will be essential for quantum technology deployment and maintenance in industry settings. This type of community college engagement could become a crucial bridge between academic quantum research and the practical skills needed to scale quantum technologies beyond the laboratory.

Insider Brief Quantum technology is often portrayed as a field staffed by people with doctoral degrees and years of academic experience. That may be how it is now, but experts are increasingly concerned that as quantum moves out of theory and into practice and out of the laboratory and into the real world, its success […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: SpinQ Technology Secures Nearly 1 Billion Chinese Yuan in Series C Funding to Scale Industrial Superconducting Quantum Computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This substantial funding round positions SpinQ Technology as a major player in the competitive quantum computing landscape, particularly significant given China's strategic push to establish quantum supremacy. The company's focus on superconducting quantum systems puts them in direct competition with industry leaders like IBM and Google, while their emphasis on full-stack solutions suggests they're building toward practical, deployable quantum computers rather than just research prototypes. With nearly $139 million in total Series C funding, SpinQ now has the capital to accelerate hardware development and potentially bridge the gap between laboratory demonstrations and industrial applications. This investment reflects the growing global recognition that quantum computing is transitioning from an academic curiosity to a critical technology with real commercial potential.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — SpinQ Technology, a leading global provider of full-stack quantum computing solutions, today announced the successful completion of its 600 million Chinese Yuan (about $83 million U.S.) Series C+ financing round. This milestone brings the company’s total Series C funding to nearly 1 billion Chinese Yuan (about $139 million U.S.) within […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Coinbase CEO Addresses ‘Quantum Threat’ to Cryptocurrencies
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a significant shift in how the cryptocurrency industry views quantum computing threats, with Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong's decision to personally lead quantum-resistance efforts representing a move from theoretical concern to immediate action. The timing aligns with recent breakthroughs in quantum error correction from companies like Google and IBM, which are bringing fault-tolerant quantum computers closer to reality and making cryptographic vulnerabilities more tangible. While current quantum computers can't break Bitcoin's encryption, Armstrong's proactive stance reflects growing industry awareness that quantum-resistant cryptographic standards need implementation years before quantum computers become capable enough to pose actual threats. This development highlights how quantum computing progress is now influencing real-world infrastructure decisions across major technology sectors.

Insider Brief A steady stream of announcements that quantum teams are making progress on quantum error correction seems to have stirred the once-skeptical cryptocurrency community. Coinbase CEO and co-founder Brian Armstrong said he will personally lead an industry push to make Bitcoin quantum-resistant, marking a shift from long-term concern to near-term engineering priority after new […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuEra Launches Open-Source Package to Simulate Logical Quantum Circuits at Scale
item.description:
Curator's Take: This open-source T-gate simulator from QuEra represents a crucial breakthrough in quantum error correction development, as simulating non-Clifford operations like T-gates has been a major computational bottleneck that limited researchers' ability to test logical quantum circuits at realistic scales. The GPU acceleration addresses a fundamental challenge in the field - while Clifford gates can be simulated efficiently using classical computers, T-gates require exponentially more resources, making large-scale QEC testing practically impossible until now. By democratizing access to scalable logical circuit simulation, this tool could accelerate the development of fault-tolerant quantum algorithms and help bridge the gap between today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and tomorrow's error-corrected quantum computers. The timing is particularly significant as the quantum computing field increasingly focuses on achieving practical quantum advantage through error correction rather than just building larger noisy systems.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QuEra Computing today open-sourced its T-gate simulator (Tsim), a GPU-accelerated quantum circuit simulator that, for the first time, lets researchers simulate non-Clifford gate operations at the speed and scale that quantum error correction (QEC) development demands. QEC is the essential bridge between today’s noisy quantum processors and the fault-tolerant machines […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum entanglement between electrons and ions captured at attosecond timescale
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents a remarkable achievement in quantum measurement technology, capturing the fleeting moments when quantum entanglement forms between electrons and ions on attosecond timescales - that's quintillionths of a second. The ability to observe entanglement at such ultrafast timescales opens new windows into understanding how quantum correlations emerge and evolve in real-time, which could be crucial for developing more robust quantum technologies. This research bridges fundamental quantum physics with practical applications, as better understanding of entanglement dynamics could lead to improvements in quantum computing error correction and quantum communication protocols. The work demonstrates how advances in attosecond measurement techniques are pushing the boundaries of what we can observe in the quantum world, potentially revealing new physics that occurs in these incredibly brief moments.

Quantum mechanics is extremely successful at describing the behavior of matter at the atomic level. This success forces one to accept that certain aspects of physical reality go far beyond our intuition. Among these, none is more intriguing than the concept of quantum entanglement, which mathematically describes how two particles that have at some point in the past interacted with each other retain a memory of this interaction to such an extent that acting on one of the two particles has a measurable influence on the properties of the other particle, even if the two have long ago stopped interacting and may be separated by such a vast distance that communication between them is no longer possible.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Space-Efficient Quantum Algorithm for Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithms with Resource Estimation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a significant optimization for one of quantum computing's most consequential applications: breaking elliptic curve cryptography that secures much of today's internet infrastructure. The researchers achieved a 37% reduction in logical qubits needed for a 256-bit curve (from 2124 to 1333 qubits), which brings quantum cryptanalysis closer to the realm of practical feasibility as fault-tolerant quantum computers scale up. By cleverly optimizing the modular inversion operation through improved register sharing and length-based storage techniques, this work directly addresses one of the key resource bottlenecks that determine when quantum computers will pose a real threat to current cryptographic standards. This type of algorithmic efficiency improvement is crucial because it means organizations may need to transition to post-quantum cryptography sooner than previously estimated.

Solving the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP) is critical for evaluating the quantum security of widely deployed elliptic-curve cryptosystems. Consequently, minimizing the number of logical qubits required to execute this algorithm is a key object. In implementations of Shor's algorithm, the space complexity is largely dictated by the modular inversion operation during point addition. Starting from the extended Euclidean algorithm (EEA), we refine the register-sharing method of Proos and Zalka and propose a space-efficient reversible modular inversion algorithm. We use length registers together with location-controlled arithmetic to store the intermediate variables in a compact form throughout the computation. We then optimize the stepwise update rules and give concrete circuit constructions for the resulting controlled arithmetic components. This leads to a modular inversion circuit that uses $3n + 4\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + O(1)$ logical qubits and $204n^2\log_2 n + O(n^2)$ Toffoli gates. By inserting this modular inversion component into the controlled affine point-addition circuit, we obtain a space-efficient algorithm for the ECDLP with $5n + 4\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + O(1)$ qubits and $O(n^3)$ Toffoli gates. In particular, for a 256-bit prime-field curve, our estimate reduces the logical-qubit count to 1333, compared with 2124 in the previous low-width implementation of Häner et al.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Twinning! Quantum ‘Digital Twins’ Tackle Error Correction Task to Speed Path to Reliable Quantum Computers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights an intriguing approach to one of quantum computing's biggest challenges: understanding and predicting how errors propagate through large quantum systems before building them physically. By creating classical "digital twins" that can simulate error-prone quantum computers, researchers are essentially developing a testing ground to optimize error correction strategies without the enormous cost and complexity of building multiple physical quantum machines. This represents a smart convergence of classical simulation techniques with quantum error correction research, potentially accelerating the development timeline for fault-tolerant quantum computers by allowing scientists to experiment with different architectures and error correction codes computationally first. The timing is particularly noteworthy as it comes during what appears to be a breakthrough week for quantum error correction advances across the field.

Insider Brief In a week that has seen a parade of error-correction advances, a team of quantum computing researchers say they have shown a practical way to simulate large, error-prone quantum systems on classical computers, an advance that the scientists suggest could speed the path toward reliable quantum machines. A collaboration involving Amazon Web Services, […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Explicit constructions of mutually unbiased bases via Hadamard matrices
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum information's most elegant yet stubborn mathematical puzzles: constructing complete sets of mutually unbiased bases, which are fundamental to quantum cryptography protocols and quantum state tomography. The researchers provide explicit analytical methods for dimensions 2, 3, and 4, while highlighting why dimension 6 remains frustratingly resistant to complete solutions—a famous open problem that has puzzled mathematicians for decades. Their systematic computational framework bridges pure mathematics with practical quantum applications, offering tools that could improve quantum key distribution security and enable more efficient quantum state measurements. The work is particularly valuable because it translates abstract group theory and finite field mathematics into concrete constructions that quantum experimentalists can actually implement.

We present a detailed computational and algebraic study of Mutually Unbiased Bases (MUBs) in finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, with a particular focus on dimensions 2, 3, 4, and the challenging case of 6. Starting from the Hadamard-phase parametrization, we derive explicit analytical conditions for mutual unbiasedness in dimension 4, providing a tractable system of trigonometric constraints on the phase parameters. We then explore a tensor-product construction via Pauli operators, highlighting the algebraic and group-theoretical origin of MUBs in two-qubit systems, and demonstrating how these constructions yield a complete set of 5 MUBs in dimension 4. Extending our approach, we investigate the Fourier-family method in dimension 6, where the absence of a prime-power structure imposes strong rigidity constraints and limits the known constructions to sets of 3 MUBs. We provide a systematic computational framework for testing candidate phase vectors, bridging the gap between analytical insight and numerical exploration. Finally, we generalize the discussion to arbitrary prime-power dimensions, emphasizing the role of finite-field structures, Heisenberg-Weyl operators, and discrete symmetries in generating complete sets of MUBs. Our work offers a transparent, line-by-line verification methodology, highlighting both the geometric and algebraic richness of MUBs, and clarifying why certain dimensions resist full analytical constructions. This study serves as a comprehensive resource for researchers seeking both theoretical understanding and practical construction of MUBs in quantum information science.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The Phase Quantum Walk: A Unified Framework for Graph State Distribution in Quantum Networks
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces an elegant solution to one of quantum networking's most persistent challenges: how to efficiently distribute complex entangled states beyond simple GHZ states across quantum networks. The phase quantum walk framework is particularly significant because it demonstrates that arbitrary graph state topologies can be generated with identical fidelity - a surprising theoretical result that the researchers validated experimentally on IBM's latest hardware. The finding that graph topology doesn't affect distribution fidelity could dramatically simplify the design of distributed quantum algorithms and measurement-based quantum computing protocols. This work bridges fundamental quantum walk theory with practical quantum networking applications, offering a unified approach that could accelerate the development of modular quantum systems where different processors need to share complex entangled resources.

Distributing arbitrary graph states across quantum networks is a central challenge for modular quantum computing and measurement-based quantum communication. I introduce the phase quantum walk (PQW), a discrete-time quantum walk in which the conventional position-permuting shift operator is replaced by a diagonal conditional phase (CZ) gate, enabling distribution of arbitrary graph states, not merely GHZ states, from elementary two-qubit resources. The Byproduct Lemma shows that each walk step teleports edge entanglement with a correctable Pauli byproduct; the Coin Invariance Theorem proves that the optimal fidelity F*(C,E) = F*(H,E) for all unitary coins C and noise channels E, with closed-form expressions F_dep = (1 - 3p/4)^k and F_pd = ((1 + sqrt(1 - p))/2)^k. Analytical correction formulas are derived for tree graphs (general theorem) and ring graphs (C4 case study), with F = 1.0 verified across eight topologies (up to 4096 outcomes). Hardware validation on ibm marrakesh (IBM Heron r2, CZ-native) yields F_cl = 0.924 for |GHZ4> and 0.922 for |L4>, statistically identical, providing the first experimental confirmation that fidelity is independent of graph topology as predicted by the Coin Invariance Theorem.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Constrained Quantum Optimization via Iterative Warm-Start XY-Mixers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of QAOA's most persistent challenges by developing a mathematically rigorous way to combine warm-starting techniques with XY-mixers while preserving crucial convergence guarantees. The breakthrough lies in formulating warm-started XY-mixer Hamiltonians that maintain ground-state properties, enabling quantum algorithms to both respect hard constraints and leverage classical preprocessing to bias the search toward promising solution regions. The dramatic performance improvements demonstrated on classic problems like Max-k-Cut and TSP, with optimal solution probabilities increasing by orders of magnitude, suggest this could significantly enhance the practical utility of near-term quantum optimization algorithms. This work represents an important step toward making QAOA more competitive with classical heuristics for real-world constrained optimization problems.

The Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) is a leading hybrid heuristic for combinatorial optimization, but efficiently handling hard constraints remains a significant challenge. XY-mixers successfully confine quantum state evolution to a feasible subspace, such as the Hamming-weight-1 sector for one-hot constraints. On the contrary, warm-starting biases the search toward promising regions based on preliminary solutions. Combining these two techniques requires maintaining the essential alignment between the initial state and the mixer Hamiltonian to preserve convergence guarantees. Previous work demonstrated warm-starting with XY-mixers via a biased initial state, but relying only on standard mixer Hamiltonians. Consequently, the initial state is no longer a ground state of the mixer. In this work, we overcome these limitations by formulating a warm-started XY-mixer Hamiltonian for one-hot constraints and proving its ground-state properties. Furthermore, we provide a shallow circuit implementation suitable for NISQ implementations. We embed the warm-starting into a classical heuristic that iteratively updates the bias based on previous samples, called Iterative Warm-Starting (IWS). Extensive numerical simulations on Max-$k$-Cut and Traveling Salesperson Problem instances demonstrate that IWS-QAOA significantly accelerates the solution-finding process, increasing the probability of sampling optimal solutions by orders of magnitude compared to standard XY-QAOA. Finally, we validate our approach on the ibm_boston QPU using hardware-tailored 144-qubit problem instances. By coupling IWS-QAOA with a greedy steepest-descent post-processing strategy to repair infeasible measurements caused by hardware noise, we successfully identify optimal solutions on actual quantum devices.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Photonic qubit encoding interconversion for heterogeneous quantum networking
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles a crucial infrastructure challenge for the quantum internet: how to connect different types of quantum computers and devices that encode information in fundamentally different ways. The researchers demonstrated a clever workaround for transmitting polarization-encoded qubits through optical fibers by temporarily converting them to time-bin encoding, which is much more robust against the fiber's natural tendency to scramble polarization states. This interconversion protocol essentially acts as a universal translator between quantum systems, allowing polarization-based quantum computers to communicate with time-bin systems while maintaining the delicate quantum correlations needed for applications like distributed quantum computing and sensing networks. The ability to preserve Bell state entanglement across this conversion process represents a significant step toward building the modular, mix-and-match quantum networks that will be essential for scaling quantum technologies beyond individual laboratory setups.

Quantum information processing, communication, and sensing networks are being developed with various qubit platforms that use different encoding schemes. Connecting quantum network nodes to distribute entanglement requires matching photon qubit basis encoding. In this work, we implement an interconversion protocol which converts photon qubit encoding from the polarization basis to the time-bin basis, transmits the photons through a transport fiber with large fluctuations in polarization, and converts back to polarization encoding for ease of measurement. This interconversion scheme faithfully transmits a polarization Bell state across the transport fiber by converting sources of infidelity to changes in transmission rate. These results illustrate a practical approach for interfacing distinct qubit platforms to enable modular and flexible operation in heterogeneous quantum networks.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Emergence of volume-law scaling for entanglement negativity from the Hawking radiation of analogue black holes
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough demonstrates that Hawking radiation creates a fundamentally different type of quantum entanglement than what physicists typically observe in quantum field theory, potentially solving longstanding puzzles about information loss in black holes. The researchers showed that entangled particle pairs generated near the event horizon produce a "volume-law" scaling pattern that could actually be measured in laboratory experiments using ultracold atoms to simulate black hole physics. What makes this particularly exciting is that it bridges theoretical black hole physics with practical quantum simulation, offering the first concrete pathway to experimentally probe one of the most mysterious aspects of quantum gravity. The findings suggest that the entanglement structure of Hawking radiation might hold the key to understanding how information escapes from black holes, addressing one of physics' most fundamental paradoxes.

The quantum information content of Hawking radiation holds the key to understanding black-hole evaporation and the fate of unitarity. Motivated by recent advances in cold-atom experiments, we develop a lattice-regularization approach aimed at simulating the coarse-grained entanglement scaling of a quantum field in a 1+1D analogue black-hole background. We provide the first concrete demonstration that logarithmic negativity -- an entanglement monotone that typically exhibits a UV-divergent log-scaling for the conformal vacuum -- acquires a UV-finite volume term from the nonlocal correlations seeded by Hawking radiation. We show that this volume term encodes both the number density and spatial distribution of entangled Hawking pairs along the black-hole interior and exterior. We highlight its prospective detection in currently realizable experiments as well as its implications beyond the analogue paradigm, in particular for black-hole thermodynamics.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Decoding Realistic Quantum Error Syndrome with Quantum Elements Digital Twins
item.description:
Curator's Take: This AWS research tackles one of quantum computing's most critical engineering challenges: determining the precise hardware quality and code size needed to achieve practical fault tolerance. By using digital twins to simulate realistic quantum error syndromes, the work moves beyond theoretical thresholds to provide concrete guidance for building useful quantum computers. The findings offer hardware developers and quantum engineers crucial benchmarks for when their systems will cross the fault-tolerance finish line, potentially accelerating the timeline for commercially viable quantum advantage. This represents a vital bridge between quantum error correction theory and the messy realities of actual quantum hardware implementation.

Fault-tolerant quantum computing requires quantum error correction (QEC): Encoding one logical qubit into many physical qubits so that, below a threshold error rate, the logical error rate falls rapidly as the code grows. The practical engineering question is: How large must the code be and how good must the hardware be to reach a useful […]

Read original at AWS Quantum Computing Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum search algorithm for similar subgraph identification under fixed edge removal
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a quantum algorithm that could significantly impact network analysis and infrastructure modeling by solving the computationally challenging problem of finding similar subgraphs when edges are removed from a network. The researchers achieve a polynomial speedup over classical methods by cleverly using Dicke states to represent all possible graph configurations in quantum superposition, then applying amplitude estimation to identify the best match. What makes this particularly exciting is the demonstrated application to electric power grid analysis, suggesting real-world utility for understanding network resilience and failure modes. While the quantum advantage requires fault-tolerant quantum computers to be fully realized, this work represents an important step toward practical quantum algorithms for complex network optimization problems that plague many industries.

We introduce a novel quantum algorithm for similar subgraph identification in form of an NP-hard cardinality-constrained binary quadratic optimization problem. Given a weighted reference graph with Laplacian $\boldsymbol{B}$, our algorithm determines the subgraph featuring Laplacian $\boldsymbol{B'}$ on the same vertex set, but $x$ out of $N$ inactive edges, minimizing the Frobenius distance $||\boldsymbol{B} - \boldsymbol{B'}||_\mathrm{F}^2$. We represent the $\binom{N}{x}$ graph topologies by an equal-weight superposition in form of a Dicke state, enabling controlled transformations applied to the quantum state associated with the vectorized Laplacian of the reference graph. Combined with amplitude estimation and a minimum finding approach, our algorithm provides a polynomial speed up $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{N^{x}/x!}N\log\log N)$ compared to $\mathcal{O}(N^{x+1}/x!)$ of classical brute-force search algorithms. We demonstrate the application of our method on standard test cases, which represent electric power grids, by reconstructing $||\boldsymbol{B} -\boldsymbol{B'}||_\mathrm{F}^2$ from measurements and show how our approach can be additionally used to calculate energy functional like quadratic forms of the Laplacians with respect to a given vector.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Dancing to Invisible Choreography, Quantum Computers Can Balance The Noise
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: noise-induced errors that currently prevent quantum computers from scaling to truly useful sizes. The "invisible choreography" metaphor aptly captures how quantum systems must maintain delicate coherence while environmental interference constantly threatens to derail computations. What makes this significant is that noise mitigation isn't just a technical hurdle to overcome, but represents the critical bottleneck between today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and tomorrow's fault-tolerant quantum computers that could revolutionize fields from drug discovery to cryptography. The article likely explores emerging strategies for quantum error correction and noise reduction techniques that researchers are developing to finally unlock large-scale quantum computing's potential.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Large-scale quantum computers are waiting in the wings. One of the main reasons we don’t have them yet is because quantum hardware is so noisy. This isn’t the type of noise you’d want to shush in a crowded theater. When it comes to computers, noise means errors that crop up […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Infleqtion Delivers Quantum Precision Timing for Mission-Critical Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement marks a significant step toward practical quantum sensing applications, as Infleqtion's Tiqker quantum optical clock technology moves from laboratory demonstration to real-world deployment in mission-critical systems through their partnership with defense contractor Safran. The timing represents a crucial milestone for the quantum sensing sector, which has been gaining momentum as one of the most near-term viable applications of quantum technology alongside computing and communications. Precision timing is essential for everything from GPS systems and financial trading to telecommunications infrastructure, so quantum-enhanced timing solutions could provide substantial advantages in accuracy and resilience against interference. This commercial availability suggests the quantum sensing market is maturing beyond pure research into deployable technologies that could reshape critical infrastructure systems.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Infleqtion (NYSE: INFQ), a global leader in quantum sensing and quantum computing powered by neutral-atom technology, announced availability of the first quantum-enabled precision timing solution delivered as part of the company’s partnership with global civil and military equipment manufacturer Safran Electronics & Defense. The new solution includes Infleqtion’s Tiqker™ quantum optical […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuiX Quantum Demonstrates Below-Threshold Error Mitigation in Photonic Quantum Computing For First Time
item.description:
Curator's Take: QuiX Quantum's demonstration of below-threshold error mitigation represents a crucial milestone for photonic quantum computing, showing that light-based qubits can achieve error rates low enough for fault-tolerant quantum computation. This breakthrough is particularly significant because photonic systems have traditionally struggled with the high error rates that plague most quantum computing platforms, making this the first time any photonic approach has crossed this critical threshold. The achievement positions European quantum computing as a serious contender in the global race toward practical quantum advantage, while also validating photonics as a viable path alongside superconducting and trapped-ion technologies. This development could accelerate the timeline for building large-scale quantum computers that can solve real-world problems reliably.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QuiX Quantum, a leading provider of photonic quantum computing hardware, today announced it has demonstrated “below threshold” error mitigation for the first time on a photonic quantum computer, suppressing physical qubit errors to the level compatible with scalable, fault‑tolerant quantum computing. The achievement marks the first time a European company […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Towards Chemically Accurate and Scalable Quantum Simulations on IQM Quantum Hardware: A Quantum-HPC Hybrid Approach
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents a significant milestone in demonstrating the practical viability of quantum computers for molecular simulation, with IQM's team successfully computing ground-state energies for molecules as complex as water and even mapping out complete 2D potential energy surfaces on real quantum hardware. What makes this work particularly noteworthy is the combination of advanced quantum algorithms like Sample-based Quantum Diagonalization with classical techniques such as Density Matrix Embedding Theory, enabling the researchers to tackle pharmacologically relevant molecules like amantadine that would be impossible on quantum hardware alone. The ability to achieve chemical accuracy while scaling up to 16 qubits suggests we're approaching the threshold where quantum computers could provide genuine advantages for drug discovery and materials science applications. The hybrid quantum-classical approach demonstrated here offers a realistic pathway for near-term quantum advantage in computational chemistry, moving beyond simple proof-of-concept demonstrations to tackle real-world molecular systems.

We present a large-scale experimental study of quantum-computing-based molecular simulation carried out on IQM's Sirius 24-qubit superconducting processor, utilizing up to 16 operational qubits. The work employs Sample-based Quantum Diagonalization (SQD) together with the Local Unitary Cluster Jastrow (LUCJ) ansatz to estimate ground-state energies for a set of benchmark molecules, including H$_2$, LiH, BeH$_2$, H$_2$O, and NH$_3$. In addition, we introduce a Linear-CNOT variant of the Unitary Coupled-Cluster Singles and Doubles (LCNot-UCCSD) ansatz within the SQD workflow, trading higher circuit depth for reduced classical preprocessing. A comparison between these ansätze is provided, clarifying their respective strengths, limitations, and suitability for near-term quantum hardware. We further explore potential energy landscapes through 1D scans for H$_2$ and HeH$^+$ using both STO-3G and 6-31G basis sets, and for LiH and BeH$_2$ in STO-3G. Extending beyond this, we demonstrate the experimental construction of a full 2D potential energy surface for the water molecule on quantum hardware, mapped over a 32 $\times$ 32 grid in bond length and bond angle. To move beyond small benchmark systems, we combine SQD(LUCJ) with Density Matrix Embedding Theory (DMET) to compute active-space energies for a set of ligand-like molecules, as well as the pharmacologically relevant amantadine system. Across all studies, the majority of quantum-computed energies agree with reference FCI results, as well as with DMET-CASCI energies for embedded systems, to within chemical accuracy for the chosen basis sets. These results demonstrate the reliability of sample-based diagonalization approaches and underscore the potential of hybrid embedding strategies for extending quantum simulations to increasingly complex molecular systems, while also highlighting their practicality on current IQM quantum hardware.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Networking Fundamentals: From Physical Protocols to Network Engineering
item.description:
Curator's Take: This comprehensive tutorial tackles one of quantum computing's most pressing challenges: how to engineer practical quantum networks that can actually function outside the lab. While quantum networking research has largely focused on the physics of individual quantum links, this work addresses the critical gap between proof-of-concept experiments and the complex orchestration needed for real-world quantum internet infrastructure. The authors' Software-Defined Quantum Networking framework and focus on the "simulation-reality gap" represents essential groundwork for making quantum networks as robust and scalable as classical internet infrastructure. Their analysis of how physical imperfections like decoherence affect distributed quantum applications provides crucial insights for engineers working to bridge quantum hardware capabilities with practical networking demands.

The realization of the Quantum Internet promises transformative capabilities in secure communication, distributed quantum computing, and high-precision metrology. However, transitioning from laboratory experiments to a scalable, multi-tenant network utility introduces deep orchestration challenges. Current development is often siloed within physics communities, prioritizing hardware, while the classical networking community lacks architectural models to manage fragile quantum resources. This tutorial bridges this divide by providing a network-centric view of quantum networking. We dismantle idealized assumptions in current simulators to address the "simulation-reality gap," recasting them as explicit control-plane constraints. To bridge this gap, we establish Software-Defined Quantum Networking (SDQN) as a prerequisite for scale, prioritizing a symbiotic, dual-plane architecture where classical control dictates quantum data flow. Specifically, we synthesize reference models for SDQN and the Quantum Network Operating System (QNOS) for hardware abstraction, and adapt a Quantum Network Utility Maximization (Q-NUM) framework as a unifying mathematical lens for engineers to reason about trade-offs between entanglement routing, scheduling, and fidelity. Furthermore, we analyze Distributed Quantum AI (DQAI) over imperfect networks as a case study, illustrating how physical constraints such as probabilistic stragglers and decoherence dictate application-layer viability. Ultimately, this tutorial equips network engineers with the tools required to transition quantum networking from a bespoke physics experiment into a programmable, multi-tenant global infrastructure.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Topology-Hiding Connectivity-Assurance for QKD Inter-Networking
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a critical real-world challenge in quantum key distribution networks: how to verify that secure quantum connections exist between endpoints without exposing sensitive network topology information that could aid attackers. The protocol essentially creates a cryptographic "proof of connectivity" that allows QKD network operators to demonstrate their infrastructure's security capabilities to partners and clients while keeping their actual network architecture confidential. This addresses a key barrier to widespread QKD deployment, where organizations need assurance about network security but providers are reluctant to reveal operational details that could compromise their competitive advantage or create new attack vectors. The work represents an important step toward making quantum networks more commercially viable by solving the trust-versus-transparency dilemma that has hindered inter-network QKD collaborations.

While QKD ensures information-theoretic security at the link level, real-world deployments depend on trusted repeaters, creating potential vulnerabilities. In this paper, we thus introduce a topology-hiding connectivity assurance protocol to enhance trust in quantum key distribution (QKD) network infrastructures. Our protocol allows network providers to jointly prove the existence of a secure connection between endpoints without revealing internal topology details. By extending graph-signature techniques to support multi-graphs and hidden endpoints, we enable zero-knowledge proofs of connectivity that ensure both soundness and topology hiding. We further discuss how our approach can certify, e.g., multiple disjoint paths, supporting multi-path QKD scenarios. This work bridges cryptographic assurance methods with the operational requirements of QKD networks, promoting verifiable and privacy-preserving inter-network connectivity.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Topology-Hiding Path Validation for Large-Scale Quantum Key Distribution Networks
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a critical trust challenge that will emerge as quantum key distribution networks scale beyond laboratory demonstrations into real-world deployments. The clever protocol allows users to verify that their quantum-encrypted communications are actually following agreed security policies without forcing network operators to expose sensitive infrastructure details that could create new vulnerabilities. With QKD networks beginning to connect cities across China, Europe, and other regions, this "trust but verify" approach could prove essential for enterprise adoption where customers need assurance their data paths meet contractual security requirements. The lightweight overhead of just 70kB and 1-2 second processing time suggests this validation layer won't become a bottleneck as quantum networks expand to hundreds of nodes.

Secure long-distance communication in quantum key distribution (QKD) networks depends on trusted repeater nodes along the entire transmission path. Consequently, these nodes will be subject to strict auditing and certification in future large-scale QKD deployments. However, trust must also extend to the network operator, who is responsible for fulfilling contractual obligations -- such as ensuring certified devices are used and transmission paths remain disjoint where required. In this work, we present a path validation protocol specifically designed for QKD networks. It enables the receiver to verify compliance with agreed-upon policies. At the same time, the protocol preserves the operator's confidentiality by ensuring that no sensitive information about the network topology is revealed to users. We provide a formal model and a provably secure generic construction of the protocol, along with a concrete instantiation. For long-distance communication involving 100 nodes, the protocol has a computational cost of 1-2.5s depending on the machine, and a communication overhead of less than 70kB - demonstrating the efficiency of our approach.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Novel approach to quantum error correction portends a scalable future for quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough tackles one of quantum computing's most formidable challenges: the massive overhead required for error correction, where thousands of physical qubits are typically needed to create a single logical qubit capable of reliable computation. By developing a novel "gauging logical operators" approach, the University of Sydney team has potentially found a way to dramatically reduce this overhead, bringing us closer to practical quantum computers that can outperform classical systems on real-world problems. The work represents a significant theoretical advance in fault-tolerant quantum computing, addressing the scalability bottleneck that has long plagued the field. If this approach can be successfully implemented in hardware, it could accelerate the timeline for achieving quantum advantage in commercially relevant applications by years or even decades.

A University of Sydney quantum physicist has developed a new approach to quantum error correction that could significantly reduce the number of physical qubits required to build large-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computers. The study, co-authored by Dr. Dominic Williamson from the School of Physics, is titled "Low-overhead fault-tolerant quantum computation by gauging logical operators" and published in Nature Physics.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Distribution of Bell State Entanglement in Qubit Networks via Collision Models
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a clever solution to one of quantum networking's fundamental challenges: how to create entanglement between qubits that aren't directly connected to each other. The collision model approach uses environmental ancilla qubits as "quantum messengers" that sequentially interact with network nodes, enabling researchers to generate Bell state entanglement between distant qubits without requiring direct coupling between them. What makes this particularly exciting is the demonstration that even when an ancilla only touches a single node, it can still facilitate entanglement distribution across the entire network through carefully orchestrated interaction patterns. This work provides a practical framework that could significantly advance modular quantum computing architectures and quantum communication protocols, where distributing high-quality entanglement across spatially separated components remains a critical bottleneck.

We propose a general scheme to controllably distribute pairwise entanglement in a quantum network of qubits by exploiting environmental ancilla qubits interacting with the network nodes through tunable Hamiltonians. Our approach leverages collision models, in which a quantum syatem interacts sequentially with ancilla units. We explore two distinct scenarios within this framework: one in which the ancilla is reset to its initial coherent state after each interaction (the traditional collision model), and another where the ancilla is not reset but its state is simply carried over to the next interaction, which we dub the repeated interaction model. In both scenarios, we ensure that the system-ancilla correlations are discarded between steps. We also demonstrate how varying the ancilla-system interaction patterns enables selective generation of entanglement between different qubit pairs, including non-neighbouring nodes that do not directly interact. The scheme is analyzed in networks up to three qubits under both collision and repeated interaction dynamics, revealing the genaration of maximally entangled bell pairs even in configurations where the interacting ancilla couples to only a single node. Our results provide a systematic and physically implementable route to entanglement distribution, offering potential applications in quantum communication, metrology and modular quantum computing.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Trapped ion quantum technology gets smaller
item.description:
Curator's Take: This development represents a crucial step toward making quantum computing more practical and accessible by miniaturizing trapped ion technology, which has been one of the most promising approaches for building stable quantum computers. The creation of portable trapped ion devices could dramatically expand where quantum technologies can be deployed, moving beyond laboratory environments to field applications like precision navigation, geological surveying, and distributed quantum networks. While the article summary is brief, the implications are significant since trapped ions already demonstrate some of the longest coherence times and highest fidelity operations among quantum computing platforms. This miniaturization effort aligns with the broader industry push to make quantum technologies more commercially viable, though we'll need to see how much computational power can be maintained as these systems shrink.

Portable device could find applications in quantum computing and optical clocks The post Trapped ion quantum technology gets smaller appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum-Enhanced Processing with Tensor-Network Frontends for Privacy-Aware Federated Medical Diagnosis
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of the most challenging puzzles in quantum computing: how to apply quantum advantages to real-world problems that are too complex for today's limited quantum hardware. The researchers cleverly sidestep the "dimensionality curse" by using classical tensor networks to compress high-dimensional medical images into compact representations that small quantum processors can actually handle, then apply quantum enhancement to improve federated learning while preserving patient privacy. What makes this particularly significant is the practical focus on communication costs and privacy constraints that plague real medical AI deployments - showing that quantum computing research is maturing beyond theoretical demonstrations toward solving genuine industry bottlenecks. The finding that quantum enhancement effectiveness depends heavily on the classical preprocessing architecture suggests we're entering a nuanced era where hybrid quantum-classical design choices will be as important as the quantum algorithms themselves.

We propose a privacy-aware hybrid framework for federated medical image classification that combines tensor-network representation learning, MPC-secured aggregation, and post-aggregation quantum refinement. The framework is motivated by two practical constraints in privacy-aware federated learning: MPC can introduce substantial communication overhead, and direct quantum processing of high-dimensional medical images is unrealistic with a small number of qubits. To address both constraints within a single architecture, client-side tensor-network frontends, Matrix Product State (MPS), Tree Tensor Network (TTN), and Multi-scale Entanglement Renormalization Ansatz (MERA), compress local inputs into compact latent representations, after which a Quantum-Enhanced Processor (QEP) refines the aggregated latent feature through quantum-state embedding and observable-based readout. Experiments on PneumoniaMNIST show that the effect of the QEP is frontend-dependent rather than uniform across architectures. In the present setting, the TTN+QEP combination exhibits the most balanced overall profile. The results also suggest that the QEP behaves more stably when the qubit count is sufficiently matched to the latent dimension, while noisy conditions degrade performance relative to the noiseless setting. The MPC benchmark further shows that communication cost is governed primarily by the dimension of the protected latent representation. This indicates that tensor-network compression plays a dual role: it enables small-qubit quantum processing on compressed latent features and reduces the communication overhead associated with secure aggregation. Taken together, these results support a co-design perspective in which representation compression, post-aggregation quantum refinement, and privacy-aware deployment should be optimized jointly.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Distributed Variational Quantum Linear Solver
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most fundamental challenges facing near-term quantum computing: how to solve problems larger than what any single quantum device can handle. By partitioning large linear systems across multiple NISQ computers and coordinating their efforts through clever communication protocols, the authors demonstrate a path toward quantum advantage that doesn't require waiting for fault-tolerant machines. Linear equation solving is ubiquitous across scientific computing, from climate modeling to financial optimization, so scaling beyond single-device limitations could unlock practical quantum applications much sooner than expected. The approach cleverly combines variational quantum algorithms with distributed classical optimization, suggesting that hybrid quantum-classical networks might be the key to achieving meaningful quantum speedups in the NISQ era.

This paper develops a distributed variational quantum algorithm for solving large-scale linear equations. For a linear system of the form $Ax=b$, the large square matrix $A$ is partitioned into smaller square block submatrices, each of which is known only to a single noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) computer. Each NISQ computer communicates with certain other quantum computers in the same row and column of the block partition, where the communication patterns are described by the row- and column-neighbor graphs, both of which are connected. The proposed algorithm integrates a variant of the variational quantum linear solver at each computer with distributed classical optimization techniques. The derivation of the quantum cost function provides insight into the design of the distributed algorithm. Numerical quantum simulations demonstrate that the proposed distributed quantum algorithm can solve linear systems whose size scales with the number of computers and is therefore not limited by the capacity of a single quantum computer.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Useful quantum computers could be built with as few as 10,000 qubits, team finds
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough from Caltech and Oratomic represents a potential paradigm shift in quantum computing timelines, dramatically reducing the qubit requirements for fault-tolerant quantum computers from millions down to tens of thousands. The research suggests that practical quantum computers capable of solving real-world problems could emerge by 2030 rather than decades into the future, making quantum advantage achievable with hardware that's within reach of current technological trajectories. This finding is particularly significant because it addresses one of the field's greatest challenges—quantum error correction—through innovative approaches that work with fewer physical qubits while maintaining computational reliability. If validated through experimental demonstrations, this could accelerate the entire quantum computing industry and bring transformative applications in cryptography, drug discovery, and optimization much closer to reality.

Quantum computers of the future may be closer to reality thanks to new research from Caltech and Oratomic, a Caltech-linked start-up company. Theorists and experimentalists teamed up to develop a new approach for reducing the errors that riddle today's rudimentary quantum computers. Whereas these machines were previously thought to require millions of qubits to work properly (qubits being the quantum equivalent to 1's and 0's in classical computers), the new results indicate that a fully realized quantum computer could be built with as few as 10,000 to 20,000 qubits. The need for fewer qubits means that quantum computers could, in theory, be operational by the end of the decade.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Silicon quantum computer performs logical operations for the first time
item.description:
Curator's Take: This milestone represents a crucial step toward practical quantum computing, as silicon-based systems offer the tantalizing possibility of leveraging the entire semiconductor manufacturing infrastructure that powers our digital world. The achievement of logical operations—where multiple physical qubits work together to create more robust logical qubits that can correct their own errors—marks silicon's entry into the race for fault-tolerant quantum computing, alongside competitors like superconducting and trapped-ion systems. Silicon's natural advantages, including exceptionally long coherence times and seamless integration with classical electronics, could make it the dark horse candidate for building the large-scale quantum computers needed for breakthrough applications. This research validates silicon as a serious contender in the quantum computing landscape, potentially offering a more manufacturable path to the quantum future.

Silicon is ubiquitous in modern electronics, and now it is becoming increasingly useful in quantum computing. In particular, silicon's compatibility with existing chip technology and its long coherence times in silicon-based spin qubits make it a promising material for scalable quantum computing. A new study, published in Nature Nanotechnology, has demonstrated silicon's use in a logical quantum processor, representing the first of its kind.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum computer accurately simulates real magnetic materials, reproducing national laboratory data
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a crucial validation that quantum computers are transitioning from theoretical promise to practical utility in materials science. By successfully reproducing data from national laboratory experiments, this work demonstrates that quantum simulators can now tackle real-world materials problems that have historically challenged classical computers due to the exponential complexity of quantum many-body systems. The achievement is particularly significant because it bridges the gap between quantum computing research and established scientific workflows, suggesting we're approaching an era where quantum computers could accelerate the discovery of new materials for applications ranging from clean energy to advanced electronics. This successful reproduction of experimental data also provides the credibility needed for materials scientists to begin incorporating quantum simulation into their standard research toolkit.

Studying and designing novel materials is a central application of quantum mechanics. Chemists, materials scientists, and physicists focus on subtle interactions in quantum materials and to uncover them they rely on sophisticated computational and experimental techniques. Computer simulations that connect microscopic quantum interactions to measurable material properties complement experimental data to connect structure to function—but classical computers can struggle to simulate those properties. Fortunately, scientists today have a new tool in their toolbox: quantum computers.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Unlocking scalable entanglement will enable next-generation quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: creating and maintaining entanglement at the scale needed for truly transformative applications. While current quantum systems can demonstrate entanglement among dozens of qubits, achieving the stable, scalable entanglement networks required for practical quantum advantage remains a significant engineering hurdle. The emphasis on scalable entanglement is particularly timely as the field moves beyond proof-of-concept demonstrations toward building systems that can tackle real-world problems in drug discovery, materials science, and optimization. Successfully solving this entanglement scaling challenge will be a crucial milestone that determines whether quantum computing can deliver on its revolutionary promises or remain limited to specialized research applications.

Quantum computing promises to transform our world in rapid, radical and revolutionary ways: solving in seconds problems that would take classical computers years, accelerating the discovery of new medicines, creating sustainable materials, optimizing complex systems, and strengthening cybersecurity. It does so using qubits, the quantum counterparts of classical bits, which can occupy multiple states simultaneously and enable a fundamentally new kind of computation.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion
item.description:
Curator's Take: Google's decision to pursue both superconducting and neutral atom quantum computing platforms represents a significant strategic pivot that acknowledges the fundamental uncertainties still facing the field. This dual-modality approach hedges against the possibility that superconducting qubits alone may not achieve the scale and coherence needed for practical quantum advantage, while neutral atoms offer compelling advantages in connectivity and potential for larger qubit counts. The move follows similar multi-platform strategies from other major players like Amazon and IBM, suggesting the industry is maturing beyond single-technology bets toward more diversified research portfolios. This expansion could accelerate progress by allowing Google to leverage the unique strengths of each platform while cross-pollinating insights between their hardware teams.

Google Quantum AI has announced a major strategic expansion of its research roadmap, adding neutral atom quantum computing to its established superconducting hardware program. Led by founder Hartmut Neven, the move signals a shift toward a multi-platform approach to reach commercially relevant quantum advantage. While Google remains confident in the timeline for superconducting systems by [...] The post Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: New Zealand Researchers Report Advance in Optical Ising Machine for Hybrid Quantum Optimization
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research highlights an important trend in quantum computing where hybrid classical-quantum systems are emerging as practical stepping stones toward full-scale quantum advantage. Optical Ising machines represent a particularly promising approach because they can leverage quantum-inspired physics to tackle complex optimization problems without requiring the extreme engineering challenges of fault-tolerant quantum computers. The New Zealand team's focus on scalability is especially significant, as many current quantum and quantum-inspired systems struggle to maintain performance advantages as problem sizes grow. While we await the arrival of fully fault-tolerant quantum computers, these hybrid optimization devices could deliver real-world value in logistics, finance, and materials science much sooner than traditional quantum computing timelines suggest.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — With the world’s first scalable, fault-tolerant quantum computer expected to be years away, a group of New Zealand researchers has developed an exciting hybrid device that could solve big, intractable problems sooner. Research Fellow, Dr Liam Quinn of Te Whai Ao — Dodd-Walls Centre is leading the work, considered of […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Washington Begins to Treat Quantum Technology as a Strategic Priority
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a crucial shift as quantum technology moves from research curiosity to national security imperative in Washington's policy circles. The bipartisan engagement suggests lawmakers are beginning to understand quantum's dual nature as both an enormous economic opportunity and a potential threat to current cryptographic systems that underpin everything from financial transactions to government communications. What makes this particularly significant is the timing - as quantum computers edge closer to practical applications, the window for establishing regulatory frameworks, funding priorities, and international cooperation agreements is narrowing rapidly. The fact that these discussions are happening now, rather than after quantum advantage becomes widespread, suggests policymakers may finally be getting ahead of the technology curve for once.

Insider Brief Tuesday in Washington offered a clear picture of where quantum technology now stands, not as a future concept, but as a present responsibility. Meetings on Capitol Hill, organized by Optica, brought together lawmakers, federal agencies, and leaders from across the quantum ecosystem to focus on what comes next. The conversations were not about […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Paves a Two-Lane Quantum Roadmap by Adding Neutral Atom Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a major strategic shift for Google Quantum AI, which has built its quantum supremacy claims and current leadership position around superconducting qubits but is now hedging its bets by exploring neutral atom systems. The move reflects growing industry recognition that different quantum hardware approaches may excel at different types of problems, with neutral atoms potentially offering advantages in connectivity and scalability that could complement Google's superconducting expertise. By diversifying its quantum hardware portfolio, Google is positioning itself to capitalize on whichever technology proves most viable for near-term commercial applications, while also acknowledging that the path to fault-tolerant quantum computing may require multiple parallel approaches rather than betting everything on a single platform.

Insider Brief Google Quantum AI, which was a pioneer in the superconducting approach to quantum computing, announced that it is expanding beyond that core approach to add neutral atom quantum computing, a move the company says will accelerate progress toward commercially useful machines. The plan, discussed in a recent Google Quantum AI blog post, would […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Scalable quantum circuit generation for iterative ground state approximation using Majorana Propagation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles one of quantum computing's most promising near-term applications - finding ground states of molecular systems - by cleverly combining classical preprocessing with quantum circuit design. The researchers demonstrate their ADAPT-VMPE algorithm can generate practical quantum circuits for systems as large as 100 qubits while maintaining polynomial scaling, which is crucial for making quantum chemistry simulations feasible on near-term devices. What makes this particularly exciting is their focus on a real-world photosensitizer molecule used in cancer treatment, showing how quantum algorithms could eventually accelerate drug discovery and materials science. The theoretical guarantees on approximation error and the polynomial complexity bounds suggest this approach could bridge the gap between today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and useful quantum advantage in molecular simulation.

We introduce the Adaptive Derivative-Assembled Pseudo-Trotter ansatz Variational Majorana Propagation Eigensolver (ADAPT-VMPE), a quantum-inspired classical algorithm that exploits Majorana Propagation (MP) to produce circuits for approximating the ground state of molecular Hamiltonians. Equipped with the theoretical guarantees of MP, which provide controllable bounds on the approximation error, ADAPT-VMPE offers an efficient and scalable approach for iterative ansatz construction. A theoretical analysis of the computational complexity demonstrates that it is polynomial in both the number of qubits and the number of iterations. We present an in-depth analysis of circuit construction strategies, analyzing their impact on convergence and provide practical guidance for efficient ansatz generation. Using ADAPT-VMPE, we construct up to 100-qubit ansätze for a strongly correlated photosensitizer currently undergoing human clinical trials for cancer treatment. Our results demonstrate that constant overlap with the ground state across system sizes can be reached in polynomial time with polynomially sized circuits.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum simulation of Motzkin spin chain with Rydberg atoms
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a breakthrough pathway for experimentally realizing Motzkin spin chains, exotic quantum systems that have tantalized theorists for years due to their unusual entanglement properties that violate fundamental scaling laws. Unlike typical quantum systems where entanglement grows with the boundary area between regions, Motzkin chains exhibit logarithmic scaling that makes them notoriously difficult to simulate on classical computers, yet they hold deep connections to topological phases and even string theory through AdS/CFT correspondence. The researchers' proposed Rydberg atom platform offers a realistic experimental route to create these mathematical constructs in the lab, potentially opening doors to studying a whole new class of highly entangled quantum phases that could inform both fundamental physics and future quantum technologies. This work exemplifies how programmable quantum simulators are becoming powerful tools for exploring exotic quantum matter that exists at the edge of what's computationally tractable.

Motzkin spin chain is a well-known mathematical model with connections to symmetry-protected topological phases, such as the Haldane phase, as well as to concepts in the AdS/CFT correspondence. They exhibit highly entangled ground states that violate the area law and are exceptionally difficult to simulate with conventional numerical methods. Numerical simulations of the Motzkin ground state become further challenging at large system sizes due to their high-dimensional spin structure, rendering it a natural test bed for quantum simulation with ultra-cold systems. Here, we propose a Rydberg-atom based quantum simulation scheme that effectively realizes Motzkin spins using an experimentally accessible set of parameters. We show that the resulting effective Motzkin ground state reproduces the characteristic entanglement scaling and the block-structure properties of the reduced density matrix associated with the ideal Motzkin state. Our results establish a pathway toward a concrete experimental realization of Motzkin spins beyond purely mathematical constructions, opening avenues for exploring other similar exotic non-area-law entangled phases in programmable Rydberg simulators.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: NRFC Investment Accelerates SQC’s Atomic-Scale Manufacturing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This investment marks a significant milestone for Silicon Quantum Computing's unique atomic-scale manufacturing approach, which positions them as the sole company worldwide capable of fabricating quantum chips with atomic precision. The AUD$20 million from Australia's National Reconstruction Fund Corporation not only validates SQC's silicon-based quantum technology but also strengthens Australia's position in the global quantum race, particularly in the critical hardware manufacturing sector where precision at the atomic level remains one of the field's greatest technical challenges. What makes this particularly noteworthy is SQC's claim of already delivering quantum-enhanced AI products to enterprise and government clients today, suggesting their technology has moved beyond pure research into practical applications. This combination of manufacturing precision and commercial readiness could accelerate the timeline for practical quantum computing deployment, especially in applications where silicon-based quantum systems offer advantages over other qubit technologies.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — The National Reconstruction Fund Corporation (NRFC) has invested AUD$20 million in Silicon Quantum Computing (SQC), a global leader in the field of quantum computing. SQC is the only company in the world capable of manufacturing quantum chips with atomic precision while delivering quantum enhanced-AI products for enterprises and governments today. It is […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Global control via quantum actuators
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces an elegant solution to one of quantum computing's biggest architectural challenges: how to perform selective quantum operations without needing individual control over every qubit. The proposed quantum actuators act like smart intermediaries that can temporarily store and release energy on command, allowing processors to activate specific multi-qubit gates even when using simple global control fields that affect all qubits simultaneously. This approach could dramatically simplify the control electronics needed for large-scale quantum computers while actually enhancing their connectivity and enabling long-range quantum operations between distant qubits. The connection to quantum thermodynamics through the "quantum battery" interpretation adds theoretical depth and suggests these actuators might serve dual purposes in future quantum processors.

We introduce the concept of quantum actuators as mediators for globally controlled quantum computation. Auxiliary quantum systems act as controllable elements that transiently store and release interaction energy, enabling the selective activation of multi-qubit gates within globally driven architectures. During compilation they remain passive and require no fine-grained local control, while during operation they allow for controlled activation of interactions and directional flow of quantum information. We provide a framework for embedding quantum actuators in globally controlled processors, showing how they enhance connectivity, enable long-range entangling operations, and bridge distant regions without increasing local control overhead. We discuss physical implementations and architectural strategies illustrating how these elements extend the capabilities of global-control schemes. A complementary interpretation in terms of quantum batteries naturally emerges, connecting global-control architectures with concepts from quantum thermodynamics while highlighting the distinct operational role of quantum actuators.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: UK Lawmaker Proposes U.K.-U.S. Quantum Partnership Can be a Strategic Counterweight to China
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights how quantum computing is increasingly viewed through the lens of national security and international competition, with policymakers recognizing that quantum supremacy could reshape global power dynamics. Tugendhat's proposal for deeper U.K.-U.S. quantum collaboration reflects growing concerns about China's substantial investments in quantum research and the strategic importance of maintaining Western leadership in this critical technology. The mention of Quantinuum as a model partnership underscores how the quantum industry is already operating across borders, suggesting that formal government partnerships could amplify existing commercial and research ties. This signals a shift from viewing quantum computing as purely a scientific endeavor to recognizing it as a cornerstone of future economic and military competitiveness.

Insider Brief A transatlantic partnership between the U.S. and the U.K. could serve as a fulcrum in the global effort to develop quantum computing, with Quantinuum positioned as a leading example of how combined capabilities may accelerate progress and shape geopolitical competition. In a Wall Street Journal opinion column, Tom Tugendhat, a member of the […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Study Finds More Qubits Yield Better Financial Optimization Outcomes
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents a crucial milestone in quantum computing's journey from laboratory curiosity to commercial viability, demonstrating that more qubits directly translate to better real-world performance in financial optimization problems. The collaboration between IonQ and Kipu Quantum shows quantum systems moving beyond proof-of-concept demonstrations to deliver measurable improvements over classical methods in portfolio optimization, one of the most promising near-term quantum applications. What makes this particularly significant is the clear scaling behavior observed when expanding from 36 to 64 qubits, suggesting that as quantum hardware continues to improve, financial institutions may soon see compelling reasons to adopt quantum solutions. This direct comparison with classical methods provides the kind of concrete evidence that enterprise decision-makers need to justify quantum computing investments.

Insider Brief Quantum hardware is beginning to show measurable gains on real financial problems, with larger qubit systems delivering better portfolio outcomes in direct comparisons with classical methods. A blog post, detailing results from a collaboration involving IonQ and Kipu Quantum, reports that increasing the size of executable quantum subproblems — from 36 to 64 […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: What Quantum Technology Actually is and Why it Matters
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article addresses a critical communication challenge facing the quantum industry: the tendency to oversimplify "quantum technology" as synonymous with quantum computing alone. While quantum computers capture headlines, quantum sensing and quantum communications are already delivering real-world applications in everything from medical imaging to secure financial transactions, yet they often get overlooked in mainstream discussions. As governments worldwide develop quantum strategies and allocate billions in funding, having precise terminology becomes essential for effective policy-making and public understanding. The piece serves as a valuable primer for distinguishing between quantum computing's future promises and the quantum technologies already transforming industries today.

Insider Brief The term “quantum technology” has entered mainstream discourse with increasing frequency, appearing in government policy documents, corporate strategy announcements, venture capital portfolios, and media headlines. However, the phrase often gets used imprecisely, sometimes referring exclusively to quantum computing while ignoring equally transformative developments in sensing and communications. Quantum technology is not a single […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Bell Experiments Revisited: A Numerical Approach Based on De Broglie--Bohm Theory
item.description:
